body:
## Overall Assessment

This is a well-thought-out refactor: per-map caps, directional bias, deterministic spread, MOAR-style scheduling, and a runtime safety net are all genuinely useful additions. The code is well-commented and the intent is clear throughout.

**Things done well:**
- `BindMaxPmc` / `BindMaxScav` helper extraction removes repetitive config boilerplate
- One-time PMC list sort at raid start (locked distribution) — clean idea, well commented
- Scav schedule with explicit Start/Mid/Full phase points — much more controllable than the old all-at-once approach
- Runtime hard cap as a server safety net
- Good inline comments explaining intent (especially in `PickFromDeterministicIndex` and `TryUpdatePlayerPosition`)

---

### 🔴 Top 3 Priority Items

1. **Verify the `ref BotZone` Harmony signature** (`TryToSpawnInZonePatch.cs`) — if the original `TryToSpawn` does not declare `botZone` as `ref`, the patch will fail silently and the `botZone = pointZone;` assignment is dead code. This is the highest-risk item.

2. **Cache the sorted scav spawn list** (`TryToSpawnInZonePatch.FindBestGlobalSpawnPoint`) — running an O(n log n) sort over all bot spawn points on every scav tick is the biggest performance concern, especially on Streets.

3. **Unify per-map config + duplicated map switches into a single `MapSettings` lookup** — `GetMaxPmcsForMap`, `GetMaxScavsForMap`, and `GetDistanceForMap` are three near-identical switch blocks. Collapsing them eliminates the class of bugs where a new map is added to one switch and forgotten in the others.

comments.body:
🔴 **Harmony `ref` signature must match the original method exactly.**

You changed `BotZone botZone` to `ref BotZone botZone`. Harmony patches require the `ref`-ness of every parameter to match the target method's signature. If `BotSpawner.TryToSpawn` does **not** declare `botZone` as `ref`, the patch will fail to apply silently (Harmony logs a warning but doesn't throw), and the `botZone = pointZone;` assignment below becomes a dead local write — the caller's value is never updated.

Please verify this against the SPT `BotSpawner.TryToSpawn` method signature. If the original isn't `ref`, remove `ref` here and reassign the zone via a different mechanism (e.g. `__instance`'s active zone property).

🔴 **Jitter can produce a negative spawn time.**

```csharp
bossDefaultData[0].Time = currentTime + randomUtil.GetInt(-jitter, jitter);
```

`currentTime` starts at `firstWaveTimer` (currently 500 s), so in practice the subtraction is safe — but if a user ever reduces `firstWaveTimer` below 15, or if a future code path reuses this with a lower starting time, the result becomes negative and the server scheduler may behave unexpectedly.

Suggest clamping:
```csharp
bossDefaultData[0].Time = Math.Max(0, currentTime + randomUtil.GetInt(-jitter, jitter));
```

🔴 **Piecewise lerp is not guarded against misconfiguration.**

`AcceptableValueRange` prevents values outside [0,1] but does not enforce `startBudget ≤ midBudget` or `midTime < fullTime`. If a user sets `Mid Budget = 0.3` and `Start Budget = 0.5`, `allowedFrac` will *decrease* from 0.5 toward 0.3 during the first phase — scavs that were allowed early get cut off, which is the opposite of the intended schedule.

Suggest clamping at the use site:
```csharp
var midBudgetSafe = Math.Max(startBudget, midBudget); // never go below start
var fullTimeSafe  = Math.Max(midTime + 0.001, fullTime); // avoid zero-length final segment
```
Then use `midBudgetSafe` / `fullTimeSafe` in the lerp calculations.

🔴 **Random player pick each tick can cause `TravelDirection` oscillation.**

```csharp
var player = alivePlayers[UnityEngine.Random.Range(0, alivePlayers.Count)];
```

With two or more players moving in opposite directions, the selected reference player can flip on every 2-minute update, causing `TravelDirection` to swing 180°. Scavs will then spawn behind one cohort of players half the time.

Consider pinning to a single reference player for the duration of the position history window — e.g. always pick `alivePlayers[0]` (the local human player), or stash the chosen player reference and only re-pick if they die or disconnect.

⚠️ **All starting PMC ranges dropped to `{min: 1, max: 2}` — intentional?**

Every map was previously 5–13 starting PMCs; now they're all capped at 1–2. Combined with `maxTotalPmcs` (e.g. 12 on Customs), the wave budget becomes `12 − 2 = 10` PMCs scheduled across waves starting at `firstWaveTimer: 500 s`. With `taperEndPercent: 0.7`, the active window closes at ~`0.7 × raidDuration`. On a 45-minute Customs raid this means waves stop scheduling around 31 minutes in, with most PMCs appearing only after the first ~8 minutes.

Players will experience very quiet early raids. If this is intentional (encouraging a slower, more tactical early game), that's a valid design choice — but please call it out explicitly in the PR description so other contributors understand the intent.

🟡 **Duplicate map-name switch — suggest collapsing into a shared `MapSettings` lookup.**

`GetMaxPmcsForMap` (here), `GetMaxScavsForMap` (`NonWavesSpawnSystemPatch.cs`), and `GetDistanceForMap` (also in this file) are three near-identical switch blocks over the same set of map names. Any new map or rename must be updated in all three places.

Suggest a shared `Utility.GetMapSettings(string mapName)` returning a lightweight struct/record:
```csharp
public record MapSettings(int MaxPmcs, int MaxScavs, float SpawnDistance);
```
All three current methods become one-liners against the returned record. Mismatches between the three switches become impossible.

🟢 **Unconditional `LogInfo` will flood raid logs.**

This `LogInfo` fires every time a PMC wave is rejected after the cap is reached, which can happen frequently in the second half of a raid. Consider wrapping in `if (Plugin.DebugLogging)` (as you do on line 108 and 213) to keep production logs quiet:
```csharp
if (Plugin.DebugLogging)
    Logger.LogInfo($"[ABPS] PMC cap reached for {location}: ...");
```
The same recommendation applies to the other unconditional `LogInfo` calls in this file (e.g. lines 34, 87, 100, 126, 143).

🟢 **`UsedSpawnPointIds` is declared and cleared but never read or written elsewhere.**

```csharp
public static readonly HashSet<string> UsedSpawnPointIds = new(); // Track used spawn points
```

A `Clear()` call exists in `InitializeSpawnPoints`, but no code ever adds to or reads from this set. Either wire it up — for example, have `FindBestGlobalSpawnPoint` skip points whose `Id` is in `UsedSpawnPointIds` and add each chosen point after selection — or remove it to avoid confusion about whether exclusion is actually active.

🟢 **Flat global search dropped all hotzone preference for scavs.**

The old patch applied a tighter spawn distance (10 m vs 40 m) when the selected zone was a hotzone, giving hotzones denser, more aggressive scav coverage. The new flat search removed this entirely — hotzones are now treated identically to any other zone.

If hotzone differentiation is still desired, the cleanest retrofit is a score multiplier in `GetDirectionalScore` (or applied after scoring): points inside the current map's hotzone receive a score multiplier of e.g. `0.7` so they sort ahead of equally-distant non-hotzone points, recreating the preference without reintroducing zone-gated logic.

🟢 **`jitter` is a good name but consider exposing it to config.**

```csharp
const int jitter = 15;
```

A ±15 s window is reasonable, but users who prefer tighter, more predictable wave timing (or want to increase variance for a more chaotic feel) currently can't adjust it without recompiling. Since you're already exposing `firstWaveTimer`, `taperEndPercent`, and group settings, `waveTimeJitter` would fit naturally alongside them in `WaveConfig`.
